project_title: Prepare a Monthly HR Ops Report

steps.q: Step 1: It's the 1st of the month. You need to pull data for the HR deck. What is the foundational metric of any HR report?

options.text: Current Total Headcount across all departments, split by full-time vs. contingent workers.
The number of birthday cakes bought.
The total number of job applications received all year.
The CEO's daily agenda.

steps.q: Step 2: You need to calculate the Attrition Rate. You started with 500 headcount, ended with 520, and 15 people resigned. What's the denominator?

options.text: The Average Headcount for the month (510).
The Starting Headcount only.
The people who resigned.
The total number of interviews.

steps.q: Step 3: What segment of attrition data should you prioritize visualizing for the executive team to show risk?

options.text: Regrettable Attrition—the loss of high-performers or critical-impact roles.
Non-regrettable attrition of poor performers.
The attrition of summer interns.
Attrition grouped by alphabetical order of names.

steps.q: Step 4: Looking at Talent Acquisition metrics, what does 'Time-to-Fill' actually measure?

options.text: The number of days between an open requisition being approved and the candidate formally accepting the offer.
The time it takes to write a Job Description.
The duration of the candidate's first interview.
The time it takes to fire someone.

steps.q: Step 5: You notice the Time-to-Fill in Engineering has spiked from 30 days to 65 days. What is your diagnostic action?

options.text: Audit the funnel to see if the bottleneck is top-of-funnel sourcing or delays in manager interview feedback.
Blame the recruiters and move on.
Assume there are no engineers left in the world.
Delete the metric from the report to look good.

steps.q: Step 6: To report on Diversity, what data point should you include alongside the current demographic breakdown?

options.text: The diversity ratios within the hiring funnel (applicants vs. offers) to highlight pipeline equity.
Just a picture of diverse people.
The exact home addresses of all employees.
Only data from 5 years ago.

steps.q: Step 7: The CFO wants to see HR's cost-efficiency. Which metric should you add to the 'Financials' section of your report?

options.text: Cost-Per-Hire, breaking down agency fees, job board spend, and internal referral bonuses.
The cost of the office coffee machine.
The CEO's travel expenses.
The total revenue of the company's main product.

steps.q: Step 8: You import the raw CSV into Tableau/Excel. Design-wise, how should you present the data to the executive board?

options.text: Using highly visual scorecards displaying MoM (Month-over-Month) trends accompanied by a single-sentence insight.
A 40-column spreadsheet in size 8 font.
A pie chart with 30 different slices.
No charts, just a wall of text.

steps.q: Step 9: You spot a 'Red Flag': 4 employees resigned from the Sales team in one week, all citing identical leadership issues. You must:

options.text: Include this cluster in the Executive Summary with a recommended action plan (e.g., immediate skip-level meetings).
Hide it so the Sales VP doesn't get mad.
Email the whole company about it.
Wait for next month to see if it fixes itself.

steps.q: Step 10: When presenting the report in the live executive meeting, what is your role as the HR representative?

options.text: To narrate the 'why' behind the numbers, anchoring the data to business outcomes and proposing strategic interventions.
Just read the numbers off the slides monotonously.
Argue violently if anyone questions the data.
Hand out the paper and leave the room.
